prompt.title: Welcome!
prompt.description: Looks like it's your first time here. If you're new to radio astronomy, the guided observation will walk you through how to detect the hydrogen line. Otherwise, take a tour of the controls or simply explore!

trace.title: The trace
trace.description: This graph displays the total signal received by the radio telescope over the last 60 seconds.

hydrogenMarker.title: Reference Frequency
hydrogenMarker.description: Neutral hydrogen atoms in a lab will emit radio waves at exactly 1420.406 MHz. This can be used as a reference to determine doppler shift.

measurements.title: The measurements
measurements.description: Information about any detected hydrogen signal will be displayed here, including its relative velocity.

baselineCorrection.title: Baseline correction
baselineCorrection.description: Receivers have their own bandpass shape even with no signal. Capturing a baseline on empty sky subtracts that shape, and any local interference, so the hydrogen line stands out.

waterfall.title: The waterfall
waterfall.description: Open this to see signal strength stacked over time. A genuine hydrogen line shows up as a persistent vertical streak; random noise flickers and never lines up.

welcome.title: Welcome
welcome.description: This is a short tour of the telescope control panel. Use Next/Back to step through, or press Esc to exit at any time.

statusBar.title: Status bar
statusBar.description: Connection state, telescope time, and - when you are in control - your session timer live up here.

spectrum.title: Spectrum
spectrum.description: Live FFT from the SDR. The rolling average is configured on the server; watch for the hydrogen line near 1420 MHz.

manualJog.title: Manual jog
manualJog.description: Press and hold a direction to nudge the dish. The center button is an emergency stop for all motion, and the speed presets set how fast it moves.

gotoTarget.title: Go to a target
gotoTarget.description: Type a target's RA (hours) and Dec (degrees) - the coordinates any star chart or catalogue lists - then hit the arrow to drive the dish there automatically.

skyMapDesktop.title: Sky map
skyMapDesktop.description: Live view of the sky from the telescope's location. Click a target on the map, then hit the Slew button that appears to point the dish there.

skyMapMobile.title: Sky map
skyMapMobile.description: Live view of the sky from the telescope's location. Tap a target on the map, then hit Slew to point the dish there.

telemetry.title: Telemetry
telemetry.description: Encoder positions, motor currents, voltages, and safety state. Watch here if a move feels wrong - overcurrent trips show up immediately.

done.title: You are set
done.description: That covers the main sections. You can re-run this tour anytime from the Help button in the top bar.

intro.title: Observe the Milky Way
intro.description: In a few minutes we'll detect the faint 1420 MHz radio glow of hydrogen gas in our own galaxy. First we'll teach the receiver what empty sky looks like, then swing onto the Milky Way and watch the signal rise.

liveSignal.title: The live radio signal
liveSignal.description: This trace is the raw radio energy the dish hears right now. It's red and lumpy because it's swamped by the receiver's own bandpass shape and local interference - not the sky. The vertical green marker sits at 1420.4 MHz, exactly where hydrogen emits.

baseline.title: First, capture a baseline
baseline.description: To uncover the faint hydrogen line we have to subtract the receiver's own fingerprint. Click Capture baseline → below to point at empty sky and record it. I'll wait right here - the guide picks back up on its own the moment your baseline is saved.

corrected.title: Baseline applied
corrected.description: Your spectrum is baseline-corrected now: the trace is flat and green, so anything left poking up is real sky rather than the receiver's shape. The readouts above will lock onto a hydrogen line the instant we find one.

milkyWay.title: Now aim at the Milky Way
milkyWay.description: This is the live sky over the telescope. The shaded band running across it is the Milky Way - our galaxy's disk, dense with hydrogen. Click anywhere along that band to set a target, then hit the Slew button that appears to drive the dish there. Once it settles, watch the spectrum for a bump near the green marker. Take your time, then click Continue.

done.title: You found hydrogen
done.description: That bump is hydrogen gas tens of thousands of light-years away. If it sits slightly off the green marker, that gas is moving toward or away from us - the Doppler velocity under the chart says how fast. Slew to other points along the shaded Milky Way band on the sky map to compare how the gas moves across the galaxy.

lesson.title: Receivers have a shape
lesson.body: Even pointed at empty sky with no signal at all, a real antenna's spectrum isn't flat. The receiver adds its own bandpass dome, and local interference (RFI) pokes through as sharp spikes.

lesson.title: Real signals hide in it
lesson.body: Out on the sky, a faint feature like the 21 cm hydrogen line rides on top of that exact same shape - so weak it's nearly lost in the bandpass curve.

lesson.title: Divide out the shape
lesson.body: Baseline correction stores a reference trace of the receiver's shape, then divides it out of the live data. The bandpass and the RFI spikes show up in both traces, so they cancel.

lesson.title: The line stands out
lesson.body: What's left is the real sky: the hydrogen line standing on its own, instead of getting buried in the receiver's curve. Next we'll capture a baseline by pointing somewhere quiet and freezing the trace.

pick.title: Point at an empty patch of sky
pick.description: Slew the dish somewhere quiet - high in the sky, away from the Sun and the shaded Milky Way band. Click a point on the map to load it as your target, then hit the Slew button to drive the dish there. Continue unlocks once the telescope is actually pointed at a valid spot.

capture.body: Capture a baseline from this empty patch. The telescope averages one full integration window, then applies it automatically.
capture.countdownPrefix: Capturing... averaging the live spectrum over

done.body: Done - the live spectrum is now drawn with this baseline corrected. The bandpass curve should flatten out, so anything left poking up is a real feature in the sky rather than the receiver's shape.
done.meta: Look around 1420.4 MHz: a narrow bump that persists as a vertical streak in the waterfall is the hydrogen line.
